findings.description: Inverted comparison: _is_expired returns True while the entry is still fresh (age < ttl), so get() evicts fresh L1 entries and serves truly expired entries as hits indefinitely.

pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis allows arbitrary code execution if any other writer or a compromised Redis plants a crafted payload (CWE-502); a safe serializer (json, already imported) should be preferred.

RedisClient.setex silently ignores ttl_seconds, so L2 entries never expire in this implementation, contradicting the module's 'TTL-based expiry' contract even granting that the fixture elides networking.
get() cannot distinguish a cached None from a miss, so get_or_compute() re-runs compute() and rewrites both tiers on every call whenever the computed value is None.
With no locking, two threads can both observe the same expired entry and the second 'del self._local[key]' raises KeyError; self._local.pop(key, None) would be race-safe.
_local has no size bound and expired entries are removed only when read, so long-running processes with many distinct keys grow L1 memory without limit.
set() populates L1 before pickling/writing to Redis, so a serialization or Redis failure leaves the value served locally but absent from L2 (tier divergence as a side effect of a failed call).
get_or_compute() has no single-flight or locking, so concurrent misses on the same key all execute compute() simultaneously (cache stampede / dogpile effect).
invalidate() clears only this process's L1, so other instances sharing the same Redis keep serving the stale value from their local tier until TTL (no cross-instance invalidation such as pub/sub).

stats.local_entries is refreshed only in set(), so it goes stale after invalidate() or expiry deletions, and export_stats() bypasses it with len(self._local), leaving the dataclass field inconsistent/dead.
An L2 hit is re-stored in L1 with a fresh timestamp instead of the remaining Redis TTL, so a value can be served up to 2x ttl after its original write.
Expiry age is computed from the time.time() wall clock, which misbehaves under system clock adjustments; time.monotonic() is the correct source for measuring age.
ttl_seconds is not validated, so zero or negative values are accepted (once the inverted check is fixed they make every entry instantly expired; with the current bug they make every entry immortal).
warm() issues one synchronous setex per entry with no pipelining or batching, costing a network round-trip per key against a real Redis.
Keys are used verbatim with no application prefix or schema-version namespace, so collisions with other Redis tenants or stale-format values feed unexpected bytes directly into pickle.loads.
The module-level logger is created but never used; failures and notable events (pickle errors, evictions) are never logged.
CacheStats counts L1 and L2 hits in a single counter, losing tier-level observability that a two-tier cache normally exposes.
Return annotation Optional[Any] is redundant (Any already includes None) and obscures that None is also a legitimate cached value.
